annotations.label: Homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A homomorphism is a map between two algebraic structures of the same type

annotations.note: Mathlib has no single universal 'homomorphism' definition but realizes the concept per-structure via bundled hom types and `*HomClass`/`FunLike` predicates like MonoidHomClass.

annotations.label: Preservation of an n-ary operation

anchor.snippet: preserves an operation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general universal-algebra signature framework expressing preservation of an arbitrary n-ary operation; preservation is stated per-operation (e.g. map_mul).

annotations.label: Preservation of constants and identity elements

anchor.snippet: The operations that must be preserved by a homomorphism include 0-ary operations

annotations.note: `map_one` (and dually `map_zero`) state that any OneHomClass/ZeroHomClass map preserves the nullary identity/constant element.

annotations.label: Semigroup homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A semigroup homomorphism is a map between semigroups that preserves the semigroup operation

annotations.note: `MulHom` (M →ₙ* N) is a map with `map_mul'` preserving the multiplication on `Mul`/semigroup types.

annotations.label: Monoid homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A monoid homomorphism is a map between monoids that preserves the monoid operation

annotations.note: `MonoidHom` (M →* N) preserves both multiplication and the identity element between monoids.

annotations.label: Group homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A group homomorphism is a map between groups that preserves the group operation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no separate GroupHom; group homomorphisms are `MonoidHom` instantiated at group types (inverses are preserved automatically).

annotations.label: Ring homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A ring homomorphism is a map between rings that preserves the ring addition

annotations.note: `RingHom` (α →+* β) bundles a monoid hom and an add-monoid hom, preserving multiplication, addition, and 1.

annotations.label: Rng homomorphism

anchor.snippet: If the multiplicative identity is not preserved, one has a rng homomorphism

annotations.note: `NonUnitalRingHom` preserves addition and multiplication but not necessarily the unit, exactly the rng-homomorphism notion.

annotations.label: Linear map

anchor.snippet: A linear map is a homomorphism of vector spaces

annotations.note: `LinearMap` formalizes (semilinear) maps preserving addition and scalar multiplication, covering vector-space homomorphisms as a special case.

annotations.label: Module homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A module homomorphism , also called a linear map between modules , is defined similarly

annotations.note: `LinearMap` is defined precisely as a module homomorphism preserving addition and the module's scalar action.

annotations.label: Algebra homomorphism

anchor.snippet: An algebra homomorphism is a map that preserves the algebra operations

annotations.note: `AlgHom` (A →ₐ[R] B) is a ring hom commuting with the R-algebra scalar structure.

annotations.label: All operations must be preserved

anchor.snippet: a map that preserves only some of the operations is not a homomorphism of the structure

annotations.note: This is a meta-level remark about the definition rather than a formal statement provable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential function as group homomorphism

anchor.snippet: and is thus a homomorphism between these two groups

annotations.note: `Real.expMonoidHom : Multiplicative ℝ →* ℝ` captures exp turning addition into multiplication, but the codomain is not restricted to the positive-reals group.

annotations.label: Real-to-matrix ring homomorphism

anchor.snippet: then f is a homomorphism of rings, since f preserves both addition

annotations.note: `Matrix.scalar n : α →+* Matrix n n α` sends a ring element to the corresponding scalar matrix, generalizing the 2×2 example.

annotations.label: Modulus as group homomorphism

anchor.snippet: is a homomorphism of groups, since it preserves multiplication

annotations.note: `AbsoluteValue` bundles a `MulHom` (`map_mul'`) so the modulus' multiplicativity is captured, though not packaged as a group hom into the positive reals.

annotations.label: Modulus not a ring homomorphism

anchor.snippet: Note that f cannot be extended to a homomorphism of rings

annotations.note: This negative example (modulus fails to preserve addition) is not formalized as a stated lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monoid homomorphism diagram

anchor.snippet: the diagram shows a monoid homomorphism

annotations.note: The illustrative concept is just a `MonoidHom`; the specific diagram example itself is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Norm of a composition algebra

anchor.snippet: has a quadratic form , called a norm

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general theory of composition algebras and their multiplicative norm form.

annotations.label: Isomorphism (bijective homomorphism)

anchor.snippet: An isomorphism between algebraic structures of the same type is commonly defined as a bijective homomorphism

annotations.note: `MulEquiv` (and `RingEquiv`, etc.) bundles a bijection that is also a homomorphism, i.e. an algebraic isomorphism.

annotations.label: Isomorphism (categorical)

anchor.snippet: an isomorphism is defined as a morphism that has an inverse that is also a morphism

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Iso` is a morphism with a two-sided inverse morphism, the categorical isomorphism definition.

annotations.label: Invertible homomorphism is bijective

anchor.snippet: is bijective. In fact

annotations.note: `EquivLike.bijective` shows any equivalence-like map (including `MulEquiv`/`RingEquiv`) is bijective.

annotations.label: Bijective homomorphism is an isomorphism

anchor.snippet: is a bijective homomorphism between algebraic structures

annotations.note: `MulEquiv.ofBijective` (and `RingEquiv.ofBijective`) upgrades a bijective homomorphism to an isomorphism.

annotations.label: Homeomorphism

anchor.snippet: An isomorphism of topological spaces, called homeomorphism or bicontinuous map

annotations.note: `Homeomorph` is a bijection continuous in both directions, exactly a homeomorphism.

annotations.label: Endomorphism

anchor.snippet: An endomorphism is a homomorphism whose domain equals the codomain

annotations.note: `Monoid.End M := M →* M` (and `CategoryTheory.End`) defines endomorphisms as self-maps that are homomorphisms.

annotations.label: Endomorphisms form a monoid

anchor.snippet: form a monoid under composition

annotations.note: `Monoid.End.instMonoid` provides the monoid structure on endomorphisms with composition as multiplication and identity as unit.

annotations.label: Endomorphisms of a module form a ring

anchor.snippet: The endomorphisms of a vector space or of a module form a ring

annotations.note: `Module.End.instRing` gives the ring of module endomorphisms (composition as multiplication, pointwise addition).

annotations.label: Automorphism

anchor.snippet: An automorphism is an endomorphism that is also an isomorphism

annotations.note: `MulAut M` (and `RingAut`, `AddAut`) is the type of self-isomorphisms, i.e. automorphisms.

annotations.label: Automorphisms form a group

anchor.snippet: form a group under composition, which is called the automorphism group

annotations.note: `MulAut M` carries a `Group` instance (instance Group (MulAut M) in End.lean), the automorphism group under composition.

annotations.label: General linear group as automorphism group

anchor.snippet: is the automorphism group of a vector space of dimension

annotations.note: `LinearMap.GeneralLinearGroup R M := (Module.End R M)ˣ` is exactly the automorphism group of a module, equiv to `M ≃ₗ[R] M`.

annotations.label: Monomorphism (injective homomorphism)

anchor.snippet: monomorphisms are commonly defined as injective homomorphisms

annotations.note: Injectivity is `Function.Injective`, and `mono_iff_injective` identifies injective maps with categorical monos in concrete categories.

annotations.label: Monomorphism (left cancelable)

anchor.snippet: a monomorphism is defined as a morphism that is left cancelable

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Mono` is defined exactly as the left-cancellation property (f∘g = f∘h ⟹ g = h).

annotations.label: Equivalence of monomorphism definitions

anchor.snippet: These two definitions of monomorphism are equivalent for all common algebraic structures

annotations.note: Mono ↔ injective is proven for concrete categories under hypotheses (and in `Types`), but not as one universal statement over all algebraic structures.

annotations.label: Split monomorphism

anchor.snippet: A split monomorphism is a homomorphism that has a left inverse

annotations.note: `SplitMono`/`IsSplitMono` is a morphism admitting a retraction (left inverse).

annotations.label: Split monomorphism is a monomorphism

anchor.snippet: A split monomorphism is always a monomorphism

annotations.note: `IsSplitMono.mono` (dual of `IsSplitEpi.epi`) is the instance that every split mono is a mono.

annotations.label: Injective homomorphism is left cancelable

anchor.snippet: An injective homomorphism is left cancelable

annotations.note: `ConcreteCategory.mono_of_injective` shows an injective morphism is a (left-cancelable) mono in any concrete category.

annotations.label: Free object

anchor.snippet: is a pair consisting of an algebraic structure

annotations.note: Mathlib has specific free objects (FreeMonoid, FreeGroup,…) and free-forgetful adjunctions, but no single general 'free object' definition over an arbitrary variety.

annotations.label: Left cancelable homomorphism is injective

anchor.snippet: exists, then every left cancelable homomorphism is injective

annotations.note: Mathlib proves mono ⟹ injective for concrete categories under a pullback-preservation hypothesis rather than the free-object-on-one-generator condition stated here.

annotations.label: Existence of a free object on a variety

anchor.snippet: For building a free object over

annotations.note: There is no general universal-algebra 'variety' framework in Mathlib, so the general free-object existence theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Epimorphism (surjective homomorphism)

anchor.snippet: epimorphisms are often defined as surjective homomorphisms

annotations.note: Surjectivity is `Function.Surjective`, and `epi_iff_surjective` identifies surjective maps with categorical epis in (concrete) categories.

annotations.label: Epimorphism (right cancelable)

anchor.snippet: epimorphisms are defined as right cancelable morphisms

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Epi` is defined exactly as right cancellation (g∘f = h∘f ⟹ g = h).

annotations.label: Surjective homomorphism is right cancelable

anchor.snippet: A surjective homomorphism is always right cancelable, but the converse is not always true

annotations.note: `epi_iff_surjective` (Types) and concrete-category analogues give surjective ⟹ epi; the failing converse is witnessed elsewhere (e.g. ring localizations).

annotations.label: Non-surjective epimorphisms in semigroups and rings

anchor.snippet: include semigroups and rings

annotations.note: The ring case is witnessed by `IsLocalization.epi` (a non-surjective ring epi); the semigroup case is not formalized.

annotations.label: Inclusion of integers into rationals

anchor.snippet: The most basic example is the inclusion of integers into rational numbers

annotations.note: ℚ is the localization of ℤ, so `IsLocalization.epi` makes ℤ→ℚ a ring epimorphism, but this specific instance is not stated as a named example.

annotations.label: Localization as ring epimorphism

anchor.snippet: Every localization is a ring epimorphism, which is not, in general, surjective

annotations.note: `IsLocalization.epi` proves the localization map is an epimorphism in CommRingCat.

annotations.label: Split epimorphism

anchor.snippet: A split epimorphism is a homomorphism that has a right inverse

annotations.note: `SplitEpi`/`IsSplitEpi` is a morphism admitting a section (right inverse).

annotations.label: Split epimorphism is an epimorphism

anchor.snippet: A split epimorphism is always an epimorphism

annotations.note: `SplitEpi.epi` (and instance `IsSplitEpi.epi`) shows every split epi is an epi.

annotations.label: Summary of implications between morphism types

anchor.snippet: the last implication is an equivalence for sets, vector spaces, modules, abelian groups, and groups

annotations.note: The substantive claim (epi ⟺ surjective) is proven per-category (`epi_iff_surjective` for Types, ModuleCat, etc.) but the overall implication table is not a single Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Kernel of a homomorphism

anchor.snippet: is called the kernel of

annotations.note: `Con.ker f` is the kernel pair {(x,y) : f x = f y} of a homomorphism, the kernel-as-equivalence notion described here.

annotations.label: Kernel is a congruence relation

anchor.snippet: It is a congruence relation on

annotations.note: `Con.ker f` returns a `Con` (congruence relation), so the kernel of a monoid hom is formalized as a congruence (also `RingCon.ker`).

annotations.label: Isomorphism theorem for images

anchor.snippet: this fact is one of the isomorphism theorems

annotations.note: `Con.quotientKerEquivRange` (and `QuotientGroup.quotientKerEquivRange`) gives the first isomorphism theorem: domain/ker ≅ image.

annotations.label: Kernel characterized by identity class for groups

anchor.snippet: suffices to characterize the equivalence relation

annotations.note: `con_ker_eq_conKer` shows the congruence kernel of a group hom equals the one determined by its subgroup kernel (the identity's equivalence class).

annotations.label: Homomorphism of relational structures

anchor.snippet: is a mapping h from the domain of A to the domain of B

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Language.Hom` (M →[L] N) is a map of L-structures preserving functions and relations, exactly a homomorphism of relational structures.

annotations.label: Graph homomorphism

anchor.snippet: we obtain the notion of a graph homomorphism

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Hom` (G →g H) is an adjacency-preserving vertex map, the graph homomorphism.

annotations.label: Homomorphism on an alphabet

anchor.snippet: is called a homomorphism on

annotations.note: Strings over an alphabet are `FreeMonoid` and `FreeMonoid.lift` realizes alphabet maps as monoid homs Σ* → M, but the formal-language 'homomorphism on Σ' is not named as such.

annotations.label: ε-free homomorphism

anchor.snippet: denotes the empty string

annotations.note: The formal-language notion of an ε-free (non-erasing) homomorphism is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: k-uniform homomorphism

anchor.snippet: uniform homomorphism

annotations.note: The k-uniform (constant-length-image) string homomorphism notion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coding / projection

anchor.snippet: is also called a coding or a projection

annotations.note: The formal-language 'coding'/'projection' homomorphisms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language homomorphism is a monoid homomorphism

anchor.snippet: a language homomorphism is precisely a monoid homomorphism

annotations.note: `FreeMonoid.lift` is the natural equivalence (α → M) ≃ (FreeMonoid α →* M), embodying that string homomorphisms are exactly monoid homomorphisms, though not stated in language-theoretic terms.
